name: Hot & Spicy Banana Catsup
recipeInstructions:
1. Combine raisins, onion, garlic, and tomato paste in a container of food processor or electric blender. Whirl until smooth puree, adding a little vinegar if necessary to help the pureeing.
2. Pour into a large heavy or Dutch oven.
3. Peel bananas and cut into chunks. Puree in food processor or blender, adding vinegar if necessary, then add to mixture in saucepan.
4. Add remaining vinegar, 4 cups water, brown sugar, salt, and cayenne; stir to blend.
5. Bring to boiling over medium-high heat, stirring frequently.
6. Lower heat to medium-low. Cook, uncovered, stirring often, for 1-1/4 hours. If the mixture becomes too thick and threatens to stick, add enough water to just moisten.
7. After 1-1/4 hours, add corn syrup, allspice, cinnamon, nutmeg, pepper, and cloves. Continue to cook, stirring frequently, 15 minutes.
8. Remove from heat. To test for thickness: Spoon catsup on a chilled saucer. Chill in the refrigerator. A fingertip should leave a track when drawn through catsup. If the catsup has jellied, thin with a little extra vinegar and water, equal amounts of each. Let catsup cool slightly.
9. Working in batches, puree in food processor or blender, or push through a fine-mesh sieve. Return puree to rinsed-out saucepan.
10. Taste for hotness, adding more cayenne if desired (the flavor will become more pronounced after catsup has mellowed, so be cautious about adding more).
11. Wash half-pint or pint or other canning jars or bottles, and lids and bands in hot soapy water. Rinse. Leave jars in hot water.
12. Place lids and bands in a saucepan of simmering water.
13. Return catsup to boiling over medium heat, stirring constantly.
14. Add rum; stir to combine.
15. Ladle boiling catsup into hot, clean canning jars, leaving 1/4-inch headspace.
16. Wipe jar rims and threads clean with a clean, damp cloth.
17. Cover jars with hot lids; screw on bands firmly.
18. Process jars in boiling water bath for 15 minutes (water should cover jars by 1-2 inches).
19. Remove jars from boiling water to wire racks to cool.
20. Test for seals. Label, date, and store in a cool, dark place for up to one year. Catsup is ready to serve after ripening.
